> # xorg.conf (X.Org X Window System server configuration file)

	Identifier	"Configured Monitor"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
	Identifier	"Default Screen"
	Monitor		"Configured Monitor"
	Device		"Configured Video Device"
EndSection


I don't understand why this happens, my graphics always get messed up after I finally get it to where I am happy with it. 

It works fine for a few days, I shut down my pc and when I turn it on it still works fine. then again when i turn it off and back on again everything is messed up. I havent updated anything after I installed the drivers.... 

Also Is there a fix for ATA soft reset errors???

### Post by inobe on 2009-08-26
brian try adding this repo fom this site.

[http://www.avenard.org/media/Ubuntu_Repository/Ubuntu_Repository.html](http://www.avenard.org/media/Ubuntu_Repository/Ubuntu_Repository.html)

if you have jaunty installed do in terminal

Add the signing GPG key

```
wget http://www.avenard.org/files/ubuntu-repos/ubuntu-repos.key && sudo apt-key add ubuntu-repos.key && rm ubuntu-repos.key
```



Add repository.

For Jaunty (Ubuntu 9.04)

```
echo "deb http://www.avenard.org/files/ubuntu-repos jaunty release" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/avenard.list
```

you should get a prompt from update manager, run the update and reboot.

problems you may encounter' the previous versions of the driver may not get fully uninstalled, if that's the case open nvidia settings' it should give you instructions on what command to run, simply reboot once again.

it is important to know' when installing the nvidia driver from nvidia's site manually' a kernel update will break your graphics driver, doing it the way i described will prevent this and you will always get the latest version via system updater.

---

### Post by inobe on 2009-08-27
in terminal do

```
gksu nvidia-settings
```

change to it's actual resolution and save to xconfig.


you needed to access nvidia settings with higher privileges in order to make the settings stick.

after the changes the resolution shouldn't change after restarting.


ignore the crt thing, it will not make any difference if it said lcd.

---

### Post by inobe on 2009-08-27
if the crt thing gets under your skin' you can report the issue to nvidia.

[http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=46678](http://www.nvnews.net/vbulletin/showthread.php?t=46678)

canonical/ ubuntu cannot fix that, it is not opensource, it is a proprietary driver and the source is closed.

---

### Post by briantoumbs on 2009-08-27
Ok thank you I will do the gksu nvidia settings and change the resolution that way, every thing else has worked perfectly.

### Post by briantoumbs on 2009-09-13
> **inobe said:**
> brian try adding this repo fom this site.

[http://www.avenard.org/media/Ubuntu_Repository/Ubuntu_Repository.html](http://www.avenard.org/media/Ubuntu_Repository/Ubuntu_Repository.html)

if you have jaunty installed do in terminal

Add the signing GPG key

```
wget http://www.avenard.org/files/ubuntu-repos/ubuntu-repos.key && sudo apt-key add ubuntu-repos.key && rm ubuntu-repos.key
```



Add repository.

For Jaunty (Ubuntu 9.04)

```
echo "deb http://www.avenard.org/files/ubuntu-repos jaunty release" | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/avenard.list
```

you should get a prompt from update manager, run the update and reboot.

problems you may encounter' the previous versions of the driver may not get fully uninstalled, if that's the case open nvidia settings' it should give you instructions on what command to run, simply reboot once again.

it is important to know' when installing the nvidia driver from nvidia's site manually' a kernel update will break your graphics driver, doing it the way i described will prevent this and you will always get the latest version via system updater.
